title: Integration with external systems and APIs per requirements
outline: The concept of integration and the role of application programming interfaces (APIs).
Styles of API interaction: REST, SOAP, GraphQL and message queues.
Data formats, authentication and error handling in integrations.
Designing an integration according to requirements and a specification.
Reliability, versioning and testing of external integrations.
body: Integration is the process of connecting a developed program with external systems so that they can exchange data and jointly perform a business task. Almost no modern application exists in isolation: it retrieves data from databases of other services, sends payments through a bank gateway, obtains geographic coordinates from a map provider, or publishes messages to a notification service. The main tool of integration is the application programming interface, or API, which is a formally described contract that specifies how one program may call the functions of another.

An API hides the internal implementation of a system and exposes only a set of operations and data structures. Because of this, the developer of a client program does not need to know how the remote service stores or processes information; it is enough to follow the contract. A well-designed contract lists the available endpoints or methods, the parameters they accept, the format of the response, the possible error codes, and the rules of authentication. Following the contract exactly is what makes integration 'per requirements' rather than ad hoc.

The most widespread style of web integration today is REST (Representational State Transfer). In REST every resource — a user, an order, a document — is identified by a URL, and standard HTTP methods express the operation: GET reads a resource, POST creates one, PUT or PATCH updates it, and DELETE removes it. A request such as 'GET /api/v1/orders/42' asks for the order with identifier 42, and the server answers with a status code and a body, most often in JSON format. REST is popular because it is simple, stateless and built on the familiar mechanics of the web.

SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) is an older, stricter style that transmits XML envelopes and is described by a formal WSDL document. SOAP is still common in banking and enterprise systems because it supports strong typing, built-in security standards and transactional guarantees. GraphQL is a newer approach in which the client sends a single query describing exactly which fields it needs, and the server returns precisely that data, avoiding both over-fetching and under-fetching. The choice among REST, SOAP and GraphQL is dictated by the requirements and by the external system the program must talk to.

Beyond synchronous request-response calls, many integrations use asynchronous messaging through message queues and brokers such as RabbitMQ or Apache Kafka. In this model a producer publishes a message to a queue or topic, and one or more consumers process it later. Asynchronous integration decouples the systems in time: the sender does not wait for the receiver, load spikes are smoothed out, and a temporary outage of the consumer does not lose data because messages remain in the queue. This style suits event notifications, background processing and high-throughput data pipelines.

Data exchanged through an API must use an agreed format. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the dominant choice for REST because it is compact, human-readable and natively supported by most languages. XML is used with SOAP and in document-heavy domains. For high-performance internal services, binary formats such as Protocol Buffers used by gRPC give smaller payloads and faster serialization. The developer must serialize outgoing objects into the required format and deserialize incoming responses back into program objects, validating that the structure matches the contract.

Almost every real integration requires authentication so that the external system knows who is calling and whether the call is permitted. Common mechanisms include API keys passed in a header, HTTP Basic authentication, and token-based schemes such as OAuth 2.0, in which the client first obtains an access token and then presents it as a Bearer token on each request. Secrets such as keys and tokens must never be written directly into source code; they belong in configuration files or a secure vault and are supplied to the program through environment variables.

Robust integrations treat failure as a normal event rather than an exception. Networks drop packets, remote servers return errors, and responses arrive late. The client must inspect the HTTP status code: codes in the 2xx range mean success, 4xx codes indicate a client mistake such as a malformed request or missing authorization, and 5xx codes indicate a server-side problem. For transient failures the program should retry, but with an exponential backoff and a limited number of attempts, and it should apply a timeout so that a hanging remote call does not freeze the whole application.

A widely used protective pattern is the circuit breaker. If a remote service begins to fail repeatedly, the circuit breaker 'opens' and the client stops sending requests for a cooling-off period, returning a fallback response instead. After the timeout it lets a trial request through, and if the service has recovered it 'closes' again. This prevents a single failing dependency from cascading into a total outage and gives the external system time to recover.

Designing an integration begins with reading the requirements and the external system's documentation carefully. The developer identifies which operations are needed, what data must be sent and received, the rate limits imposed by the provider, and the security rules. From this they define an internal abstraction — often a 'client' class or a 'service' interface — that wraps all the low-level HTTP or messaging details. The rest of the application then depends only on this clean interface, so if the external provider changes, only the client wrapper must be modified.

In pseudocode a small REST client might look like: 'function getOrder(id): response = http.get(baseUrl + "/orders/" + id, headers={Authorization: "Bearer " + token}); if response.status == 200: return parseJson(response.body); else: raise IntegrationError(response.status)'. This tiny example already contains the essential elements: a base URL, an authenticated request, a status check and the conversion of a raw response into a domain object.

External APIs change over time, so providers publish versions, usually encoded in the URL as '/v1/' or '/v2/' or in a request header. A responsible integration pins itself to a specific version and monitors the provider's deprecation notices. When the provider releases a breaking change, the team updates the client wrapper deliberately, tests it, and only then switches, rather than being surprised by a silent change in behaviour.

Testing an integration presents a special difficulty: the tests should not depend on the availability of a live external service, which may be slow, rate-limited or paid. The standard solution is to use mocks and stubs that imitate the external API during unit tests, and dedicated 'contract tests' or a sandbox environment provided by the vendor to verify that the real contract is honoured. Logging every outgoing request and incoming response, with secrets masked, is essential for diagnosing problems in production.

Thus, integrating a program with external systems and APIs per requirements means studying the provider's contract, choosing the right interaction style and data format, implementing secure authentication, and building in disciplined error handling, versioning and testing, so that the integration is reliable, maintainable and faithful to the specification.
questions: What is an API, and why is following its contract essential for integration per requirements?
Compare REST, SOAP and GraphQL, and give a situation where each is appropriate.
How does asynchronous integration through message queues differ from synchronous request-response calls?
Which techniques make an integration resilient to failures of the external service?
Why should authentication secrets never be stored in source code, and where should they be kept instead?

title: Development of multithreaded and parallel applications
outline: Processes, threads and the difference between concurrency and parallelism.
Reasons for and models of multithreaded programming.
Shared data, race conditions and synchronization mechanisms.
Deadlocks, thread-safety and high-level concurrency abstractions.
Guidelines for designing correct and efficient parallel applications.
body: A multithreaded application performs several sequences of instructions at the same time within a single program. To understand it we must distinguish a process from a thread. A process is an independent program in execution with its own isolated memory space, while a thread is a separate flow of control that lives inside a process and shares that process's memory with the other threads. Because threads share memory, they communicate quickly and cheaply, but this sharing is also the source of the main difficulties in multithreaded programming.

It is important to separate two related ideas. Concurrency means structuring a program so that several tasks make progress in overlapping time periods; it is a property of the program's design. Parallelism means literally executing several tasks at the same instant on multiple processor cores; it is a property of the hardware execution. A single-core machine can be concurrent by rapidly switching between tasks, but true parallelism requires several cores. Modern processors have many cores, so writing concurrent programs is the way to actually exploit that hardware.

There are two broad motives for multithreading. The first is responsiveness: a user interface must keep reacting to the user while a long operation, such as loading a file or querying a network, runs on a background thread. The second is throughput: a computational task such as image processing or a numerical simulation can be split into pieces that run in parallel on different cores, finishing sooner. Choosing which motive applies shapes the whole design.

The operating system schedules threads onto cores. When more threads are ready than there are cores, the scheduler performs context switching, saving the state of one thread and restoring another. Context switches are not free — they cost time and cache disruption — so creating an unbounded number of threads is counter-productive. This is why real systems use a thread pool, a fixed set of worker threads that pick tasks from a queue, reusing threads instead of constantly creating and destroying them.

The central hazard of shared memory is the race condition. A race condition occurs when the correctness of the result depends on the unpredictable order in which threads execute. The classic example is two threads each incrementing a shared counter. The operation 'counter = counter + 1' is not atomic; it reads the value, adds one, and writes it back. If two threads read the same old value before either writes, one increment is lost. Such bugs are intermittent and extremely hard to reproduce, which makes them dangerous.

To prevent race conditions we use synchronization. The most fundamental tool is the mutual-exclusion lock, or mutex. A thread must acquire the lock before entering a critical section — the region of code that touches shared data — and release it afterwards; only one thread can hold the lock at a time, so the critical section is executed by one thread at a time. In pseudocode: 'lock.acquire(); counter = counter + 1; lock.release()'. Many languages provide a 'synchronized' block or a 'with lock:' construct that acquires and releases automatically, even if an exception occurs.

Other synchronization primitives serve different needs. A semaphore permits up to a fixed number of threads into a section, useful for limiting access to a pool of resources. A condition variable lets a thread wait until some predicate becomes true and be woken by another thread, which is the basis of the producer-consumer pattern. Atomic variables provide lock-free operations such as an atomic increment, implemented with special processor instructions, and are faster than locks for simple counters and flags.

Locking solves race conditions but introduces a new danger: the deadlock. A deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock the other needs and none can proceed. For example, thread A holds lock 1 and waits for lock 2, while thread B holds lock 2 and waits for lock 1. The four classic Coffman conditions must all hold for a deadlock: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. A common and effective preventive rule is lock ordering: all threads must acquire multiple locks in the same global order, which breaks the circular-wait condition.

Related pathologies include livelock, in which threads keep changing state in response to each other but make no real progress, and starvation, in which a thread is perpetually denied a resource because others keep taking it first. Good design minimizes the amount of shared mutable state, keeps critical sections short, and prefers coarse but simple locking strategies that are easy to reason about over clever fine-grained schemes that are easy to get wrong.

A cleaner alternative to sharing mutable memory is message passing: instead of many threads reading and writing the same variables, each task owns its data and communicates by sending immutable messages through thread-safe queues. This model, embodied in Go's channels and in the actor model of Erlang and Akka, removes many race conditions by design because there is no shared state to corrupt. The guiding slogan is 'do not communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating'.

Modern languages offer high-level abstractions that spare the developer much manual thread management. Executor services and thread pools, futures and promises that represent a value to be computed later, parallel streams that automatically split a collection across cores, and the async/await syntax for non-blocking input-output all raise the level at which the programmer works. A future, for instance, lets code start a computation, continue doing other work, and later ask for the result, which blocks only if the value is not yet ready.

It is worth remembering that not every problem benefits from parallelism. Amdahl's law states that the speed-up from adding cores is limited by the fraction of the program that must run sequentially. If ten percent of the work is inherently serial, then no matter how many cores are added the program can run at most about ten times faster. Furthermore, parallelizing input-output-bound work with more compute threads gives little benefit, because the bottleneck is waiting, not calculating; for such work, asynchronous non-blocking approaches are more effective.

Testing and debugging concurrent programs demands special care. Because bugs appear only under particular timings, ordinary tests may pass thousands of times and fail once in production. Developers use stress tests that run many threads for a long time, tools that detect data races, careful logging with thread identifiers and timestamps, and, above all, designs that minimize shared state so there is less to go wrong. Immutability — making data read-only after creation — is one of the most powerful safety techniques, because immutable data can be shared freely without locks.

Thus, developing multithreaded and parallel applications means understanding the distinction between concurrency and parallelism, protecting shared data with the right synchronization primitives, guarding against deadlock and starvation, preferring high-level abstractions and message passing where possible, and always measuring whether parallelism truly improves performance for the problem at hand.
questions: What is the difference between a process and a thread, and between concurrency and parallelism?
What is a race condition, and how does a mutex prevent it?
Describe the conditions that cause a deadlock and one practical way to prevent it.
Why are thread pools preferred over creating a new thread for every task?
According to Amdahl's law, what limits the speed-up obtainable by adding more cores?

title: Automation of testing and debugging per requirements
objective: To form practical skills in writing automated tests and in using debugging tools to locate and eliminate defects in program code according to given requirements.
theory: Automated testing is the execution of tests by a program rather than by a person, so that the correctness of the code can be verified repeatedly, quickly and without human error. Tests are organized into a pyramid: numerous fast unit tests check individual functions in isolation, fewer integration tests check that modules work together, and a small number of end-to-end tests check the whole system through its external interface. A unit test follows the Arrange-Act-Assert structure: it prepares the input data, calls the code under test, and asserts that the actual result equals the expected result.

Test frameworks such as JUnit for Java, pytest for Python and NUnit for C# provide a runner that discovers test functions, executes them, and reports which passed and which failed. To test a unit in isolation from its dependencies, developers use test doubles — mocks and stubs — that imitate databases, network services or the clock, so that a test is deterministic and does not depend on the outside world. Code coverage tools measure which lines of the program the tests actually exercise, helping to reveal untested branches.

Debugging is the systematic process of finding and correcting the cause of a defect. Rather than inserting random print statements, a developer uses a debugger to set breakpoints that pause execution at a chosen line, then inspects the values of variables, steps through the code line by line, and examines the call stack to understand how control reached the current point. Effective debugging is a scientific method: form a hypothesis about the cause, design an observation to confirm or refute it, and repeat until the root cause is found.
materials: A computer with an installed IDE (for example, IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm or Visual Studio) with a built-in debugger.
A unit-testing framework appropriate to the language (JUnit, pytest or NUnit).
A small sample project containing several functions, at least one of which has a deliberate bug.
A code-coverage tool and a report form for recording test results.
procedure: Study the provided sample project, identify its functions and their expected behaviour, and list the input-output pairs that describe correct operation.
Write a set of unit tests for one correct function, including normal cases, boundary cases and invalid input, and run the test suite to confirm that they all pass.
Run the tests against the function that contains the deliberate bug, observe the failing assertion, and record the difference between the expected and the actual result.
Set a breakpoint inside the faulty function, run the debugger, step through the code, and inspect the variables to locate the exact line where the value becomes incorrect.
Correct the defect, re-run the whole test suite to confirm that the bug is fixed and that no previously passing test has broken (regression check).
Measure code coverage, identify any untested branches, add tests to cover them, and record the final coverage percentage in the report.
tasks: Write at least six unit tests for a given function, covering normal, boundary and error inputs, and demonstrate that they pass.
Use the debugger with breakpoints and step-execution to locate the cause of a supplied bug, and describe in the report the hypothesis, the observation and the fix.
Achieve at least 80 percent statement coverage for one module and present the coverage report with an explanation of any lines left uncovered.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of writing automated unit tests, using a debugger to locate the root cause of defects, and verifying corrections with regression testing and coverage analysis.
questions: What is the testing pyramid, and how do unit, integration and end-to-end tests differ?
What does the Arrange-Act-Assert structure of a unit test mean?
Why are mocks and stubs used, and what problem do they solve in testing?
How does a breakpoint and step-by-step execution in a debugger help to find a defect?
What is regression testing, and why is it run after fixing a bug?

title: Code documentation and commenting
objective: To form practical skills in writing meaningful code comments and in generating structured technical documentation from documentation comments.
theory: Code documentation exists at several levels. In-line comments explain non-obvious decisions inside a function; documentation comments (such as Javadoc, Python docstrings or XML doc comments) describe the public interface of a function or class — its purpose, parameters, return value and exceptions; and external documents such as a README, an API reference and architecture notes describe the project as a whole. Good documentation lowers the cost of understanding and maintaining code, especially in a team.

The golden rule of commenting is to explain why, not what. The code itself already states what it does; a comment that merely repeats the code ('i = i + 1  // add one to i') is noise. A valuable comment records the reason for a decision, a subtle constraint, a workaround for a known bug, or a reference to a requirement. Well-named variables and functions are themselves a form of documentation, so clear naming often removes the need for a comment entirely.

Documentation comments follow a fixed structure recognized by generator tools. In a docstring or Javadoc block the author states a short summary sentence, then describes each parameter, the return value and any errors that may be raised. Tools such as Javadoc, Doxygen, Sphinx and Swagger/OpenAPI then read these comments and automatically produce browsable HTML reference documentation, keeping the documentation synchronized with the source because it lives next to the code.
materials: A computer with an installed IDE and a language toolchain (Java, Python or C#).
A documentation generator appropriate to the language (Javadoc, Sphinx or Doxygen).
A sample source file containing several undocumented functions and a few poor comments.
A style guide for comments and a report form.
procedure: Study the sample source file and identify functions that lack documentation and comments that merely restate the code.
Remove or rewrite the useless comments, replacing them where appropriate with clearer variable and function names.
Add a structured documentation comment to each public function, describing its purpose, parameters, return value and possible exceptions.
Insert a small number of meaningful in-line comments that explain non-obvious logic or design decisions, following the 'explain why, not what' rule.
Run the documentation generator to produce HTML reference documentation and verify that every public function appears with a complete description.
Write a short README section for the module summarizing its purpose and usage, and record in the report the before-and-after difference in readability.
tasks: Add complete documentation comments to at least five functions, each describing parameters, return value and exceptions.
Rewrite at least three poor comments into either useful comments or clearer self-documenting code, and justify each change.
Generate HTML documentation with a chosen tool and present it together with a concise README for the module.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of writing purposeful comments, producing structured documentation comments, and generating and reviewing technical documentation directly from source code.
questions: What levels of code documentation exist, and what does each describe?
Why should a comment explain 'why' rather than 'what', and give an example of a useless comment?
What information must a documentation comment for a function contain?
How do tools such as Javadoc, Sphinx or Doxygen keep documentation synchronized with the code?
How does good naming of variables and functions reduce the need for comments?

title: Version control and collaborative code development
outline: Purpose of version control and the difference between centralized and distributed systems.
Core Git concepts: repository, commit, branch and merge.
Collaboration workflows and remote repositories.
Merge conflicts, pull requests and code review.
Best practices and continuous integration in team development.
body: A version control system (VCS) is a tool that records changes to a set of files over time so that a team can recover any previous state, understand who changed what and why, and work together without overwriting each other's work. Before version control, developers exchanged files by copying folders with names like 'project_final_v2_really_final', which was error-prone and made collaboration chaotic. A VCS replaces this with a reliable, queryable history of the whole project.

There are two families of version control. In a centralized system, such as Subversion, a single server holds the authoritative history and developers check files out and in against it; if the server is unavailable, most work stops. In a distributed system, such as Git or Mercurial, every developer's clone contains the entire history, so most operations are local and fast, work can continue offline, and there is no single point of failure. Git has become the de facto standard, so the rest of this lecture focuses on it.

The heart of Git is the commit. A commit is a snapshot of the whole project at a moment in time, together with a message explaining the change, the author's identity and a link to the previous commit or commits. Each commit is identified by a hash computed from its contents, which guarantees integrity. The chain of commits forms the project history, and because each commit points to its parent, Git can reconstruct exactly how the code evolved.

Work in Git moves through three areas. The working directory holds the files you edit. The staging area, or index, is where you assemble the changes that will go into the next commit using 'git add'. Then 'git commit' permanently records the staged changes into the local repository with a message. Separating staging from committing lets the developer craft a clean, logically coherent commit rather than dumping every change at once.

Branching is the feature that makes Git especially powerful for teams. A branch is a movable pointer to a commit, and creating one is cheap and instant. Developers create a branch for each feature or bug fix, work on it in isolation, and leave the main branch — usually called 'main' — stable and releasable at all times. When the work is finished and tested, the branch is merged back. This 'feature branch' discipline lets many people work in parallel without disturbing one another.

Merging combines the changes from one branch into another. When the two branches touched different files or different lines, Git merges automatically. When they changed the same lines in different ways, Git cannot decide and reports a merge conflict. The developer must then open the conflicted file, where Git has marked the competing versions, choose or combine the correct result, remove the markers, and complete the merge. Conflicts are normal in active teams and are resolved by communication and care, not panic.

Collaboration relies on remote repositories hosted on platforms such as GitHub, GitLab or Bitbucket. A developer 'clones' the remote to get a local copy, 'pushes' local commits to share them, and 'pulls' or 'fetches' to receive the work of others. The remote acts as the shared point of truth for the team while each member still has a full local history. Access control, issue tracking and continuous integration are typically built around this central remote.

The pull request, also called a merge request, is the central mechanism of modern collaboration. When a developer finishes a feature branch, they open a pull request proposing to merge it into the main branch. This opens the change for code review: teammates read the diff, comment on problems, suggest improvements and ask questions. The change is merged only after it is approved and the automated checks pass. Code review spreads knowledge across the team and catches defects before they reach production.

A common source of confusion is the difference between merge and rebase. A merge preserves the true history by creating a merge commit that joins two branches, showing exactly how they came together. A rebase rewrites a branch's commits so they appear to have been made on top of the latest main, producing a clean, linear history. Rebasing is convenient for tidying local work, but the golden rule is never to rebase commits that have already been shared, because rewriting shared history disrupts everyone else's copies.

Teams adopt agreed workflows to keep collaboration orderly. GitHub Flow is simple: branch from main, open a pull request, review, and merge. Git Flow is more elaborate, with long-lived branches for development and releases plus short-lived feature and hotfix branches, suited to products with scheduled releases. Trunk-based development keeps branches very short-lived and integrates into main many times a day, relying heavily on automated tests. The right workflow depends on the team's size, release cadence and risk tolerance.

Good commit hygiene multiplies the value of version control. Each commit should represent one logical change, so it can be understood and, if necessary, reverted on its own. Commit messages should have a short, imperative summary line ('Fix null pointer in order parser') followed by an optional body explaining the reasoning. A '.gitignore' file keeps build artifacts, secrets and personal editor files out of the repository. And, crucially, secrets such as passwords and API keys must never be committed, because Git history preserves them forever even after deletion.

Version control is the foundation on which continuous integration is built. A continuous-integration server watches the shared repository, and every time someone pushes commits it automatically builds the project and runs the test suite. If the build breaks or tests fail, the team is notified immediately, so defects are caught within minutes rather than weeks. This tight loop between committing and verifying is what allows large teams to change code frequently while keeping it stable.

Beyond mechanics, version control supports investigation and recovery. Commands such as 'git log' show the history, 'git blame' reveals who last changed each line and why, 'git diff' shows what changed between two states, and 'git revert' safely undoes a commit by creating a new one. Because every state is recorded, a team can confidently experiment, knowing that any mistake can be traced and undone without losing prior work.

Thus, version control and collaborative development rest on the disciplined use of commits, branches, merges and pull requests around a shared remote repository, combined with code review, agreed workflows and continuous integration, so that many developers can build software together reliably, transparently and without losing work.
questions: What is the difference between a centralized and a distributed version control system?
Explain the roles of the working directory, the staging area and the repository in Git.
Why do teams use feature branches, and how is a merge conflict resolved?
What is a pull request, and what role does code review play in it?
Why must shared commits never be rebased, and why must secrets never be committed?

title: Adherence to architectural and design principles
outline: What software architecture is and why principles matter.
Core principles: separation of concerns, cohesion and coupling.
The SOLID principles of object-oriented design.
Foundational guidelines: DRY, KISS and YAGNI.
Architectural styles and the consequences of ignoring principles.
body: Software architecture is the set of high-level structures of a system: its major components, their responsibilities, and the ways they interact. Whereas detailed code decisions are easy to change, architectural decisions are expensive to reverse, because they shape everything built on top of them. Design principles are accumulated, distilled experience about how to arrange code and components so that the resulting system is understandable, changeable and reliable over its whole lifetime.

The most fundamental principle is separation of concerns: each part of a system should be responsible for one distinct aspect of the problem, and different aspects should not be tangled together. A program that mixes user-interface code, business rules and database access in one function is hard to test, hard to change and hard to reuse. Separating these concerns into layers or modules lets each be understood and modified independently.

Two closely related measures describe the quality of a decomposition: cohesion and coupling. Cohesion is the degree to which the elements inside a single module belong together and serve one purpose; high cohesion is good. Coupling is the degree to which modules depend on one another; low coupling is good. The guiding aim of most design principles is to achieve high cohesion within modules and loose coupling between them, so that a change in one place does not ripple unpredictably through the system.

The best-known collection of object-oriented guidelines is captured by the acronym SOLID. The first, the Single Responsibility Principle, states that a class should have only one reason to change — that is, it should have a single, well-defined responsibility. A class that both formats a report and saves it to a database has two reasons to change and should be split, so that a change to the storage mechanism does not risk breaking the formatting logic.

The second SOLID principle is the Open/Closed Principle: software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. New behaviour should be added by writing new code — for example, a new subclass or a new plugin — rather than by editing existing, tested code. This is often achieved by programming to interfaces, so that new implementations can be plugged in without touching the code that uses them.

The third is the Liskov Substitution Principle, which says that objects of a subclass must be usable anywhere the parent class is expected, without breaking the program's correctness. If a subclass overrides a method in a way that violates the parent's contract — for instance, a 'Square' subclass of 'Rectangle' that silently changes both dimensions — then substituting it causes surprising bugs. The principle warns that inheritance must preserve expected behaviour, not merely reuse code.

The fourth is the Interface Segregation Principle: clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. A large, general-purpose interface should be split into several small, specific interfaces so that each client depends only on what it actually needs. This keeps implementations focused and prevents a change to one unused method from forcing recompilation or re-testing of unrelated clients.

The fifth is the Dependency Inversion Principle: high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions. Instead of a business-logic class creating and depending directly on a concrete database class, it should depend on an abstract repository interface, and the concrete database implementation is supplied from outside. This inversion decouples the important business rules from the volatile technical details and is the foundation of dependency injection.

Alongside SOLID stand three plain, memorable rules. DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself — says that every piece of knowledge should have a single, authoritative representation, so that duplicated logic does not drift out of sync. KISS — Keep It Simple, Stupid — warns against unnecessary complexity; the simplest solution that works is usually the best. YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It — cautions against building features and abstractions for imagined future needs, because speculative generality adds cost and rarely pays off.

These principles are realized at the large scale through architectural styles. A layered architecture separates presentation, business logic and data access into stacked layers where each depends only on the one below. A client-server style splits the system across a network boundary. Microservices decompose an application into small, independently deployable services. Hexagonal or 'ports and adapters' architecture places the business core at the centre and pushes all external technologies to interchangeable adapters at the edges. Each style is a large-scale application of separation of concerns and dependency inversion.

It is essential to balance principles against pragmatism. Applying every principle to the extreme can produce over-engineered code with dozens of tiny classes and interfaces that is harder to follow than the problem warrants. Principles are heuristics, not laws; the skilled developer applies them in proportion to the size, lifespan and risk of the project, guided by YAGNI and KISS to avoid needless complexity.

The cost of ignoring these principles accumulates as technical debt: shortcuts and tangled designs that make future changes slower and riskier, much as financial debt accrues interest. A system with poor architecture becomes rigid, so small changes require large edits; fragile, so a change in one place breaks another; and immobile, so useful parts cannot be reused. Recognizing and repaying technical debt through refactoring keeps a codebase healthy.

Refactoring is the disciplined activity of improving the internal structure of code without changing its external behaviour, and it is how architectural principles are applied to existing code over time. Guarded by a solid suite of automated tests, developers repeatedly rename, extract, and reorganize code so that it steadily moves toward higher cohesion, looser coupling and clearer responsibilities, preventing the slow decay that afflicts neglected systems.

Thus, adhering to architectural and design principles — separation of concerns, high cohesion and low coupling, the SOLID rules, and the DRY, KISS and YAGNI heuristics — produces software that is understandable, testable and adaptable, provided the principles are applied with judgement and balanced against the real needs of the project.
questions: What do cohesion and coupling measure, and which is desirable in high or low degree?
State the Single Responsibility Principle and give an example of a class that violates it.
Explain the Dependency Inversion Principle and how it relates to dependency injection.
What do the DRY, KISS and YAGNI heuristics warn against?
What is technical debt, and how does refactoring help to manage it?

title: Applying design patterns in code development
outline: The concept of a design pattern and its origin.
Creational patterns: Factory Method, Singleton and Builder.
Structural patterns: Adapter, Decorator and Facade.
Behavioural patterns: Observer, Strategy and Command.
Benefits, pitfalls and appropriate use of patterns.
body: A design pattern is a general, reusable solution to a problem that occurs repeatedly in software design. A pattern is not a finished piece of code that can be pasted into a program; it is a description of the roles, relationships and interactions among classes and objects that solve the problem, which the developer adapts to the specifics of the case. Patterns capture the collective experience of the profession and give developers a shared vocabulary.

The idea was popularized in 1994 by the book 'Design Patterns' written by four authors known as the 'Gang of Four', who catalogued twenty-three patterns and grouped them into three categories according to their purpose. Creational patterns deal with how objects are created, structural patterns deal with how objects and classes are composed into larger structures, and behavioural patterns deal with how objects communicate and distribute responsibility.

Among creational patterns, the Factory Method defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which concrete class to instantiate. This decouples the code that uses an object from the code that constructs it, so new product types can be introduced without changing the client. It answers the recurring question of how to create objects when the exact class is not known until run time or is chosen by configuration.

The Singleton pattern ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it, useful for a single configuration object or connection pool. It is powerful but often criticized, because it introduces global state that makes testing harder and hides dependencies. A developer should apply it sparingly and be aware that dependency injection is frequently a cleaner alternative to a hard-coded Singleton.

The Builder pattern separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, so the same construction process can create different results. It is especially valuable when an object has many optional parameters: instead of a constructor with a long, error-prone list of arguments, the client calls a series of readable methods such as 'builder.setColor(red).setSize(large).build()'. This makes object creation clear and prevents invalid, half-initialized objects.

Turning to structural patterns, the Adapter pattern converts the interface of one class into another interface that a client expects, allowing classes with incompatible interfaces to work together. It is the software equivalent of a plug adapter for a foreign socket. Adapters are indispensable when integrating a third-party library or a legacy component whose interface cannot be changed but must fit into the surrounding code.

The Decorator pattern attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it in another object that shares the same interface. Instead of creating a large hierarchy of subclasses for every combination of features, decorators are stacked at run time: a plain data stream can be wrapped in a buffering decorator, then in an encrypting decorator, each adding behaviour while preserving the common interface. This favours composition over inheritance.

The Facade pattern provides a single, simplified interface to a complex subsystem. Rather than forcing clients to understand and coordinate many interrelated classes, a facade offers a few convenient high-level methods that hide the complexity behind them. This reduces coupling between the client and the subsystem and makes the subsystem easier to use, while still allowing advanced clients to reach the underlying classes when needed.

Among behavioural patterns, the Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object — the subject — changes state, all its dependents — the observers — are notified and updated automatically. It is the basis of event handling and of the model-view architectures used in user interfaces, where the view observes the model and refreshes itself whenever the model changes, without the model needing to know the details of the view.

The Strategy pattern defines a family of interchangeable algorithms, encapsulates each one, and lets the algorithm vary independently of the clients that use it. For example, a program that must sort or compress data can hold a reference to a strategy interface and be given a concrete strategy — quicksort or merge sort, zip or gzip — at run time. This removes large conditional statements and makes new algorithms easy to add, embodying the Open/Closed Principle.

The Command pattern encapsulates a request as an object, so that requests can be parameterized, queued, logged and undone. Each command object stores the action and its parameters and exposes an 'execute' method. This underlies undo/redo functionality, task queues and menu actions, because a history of command objects can be replayed or reversed. It cleanly separates the object that issues a request from the object that performs it.

Patterns bring real benefits: they provide proven solutions, a common vocabulary that makes designs easier to communicate, and structures that are more flexible and maintainable. Saying 'let us use an Observer here' conveys a whole design in two words to anyone who knows the catalogue. Patterns also tend to embody the design principles studied earlier, promoting loose coupling and adherence to the Open/Closed and Single Responsibility principles.

However, patterns must be applied with judgement, or they become a liability. Forcing a pattern where a simple solution suffices — sometimes called a 'pattern for its own sake' — adds needless complexity and obscures intent, an anti-pattern of over-engineering. The correct approach is to recognize the recurring problem first and then reach for the pattern that fits it, rather than starting with a pattern and searching for a place to use it. KISS and YAGNI still apply.

Thus, design patterns are named, reusable solutions to recurring design problems, grouped into creational, structural and behavioural families, and their thoughtful application gives a developer a shared vocabulary and flexible, maintainable structures — provided each pattern is chosen because the problem genuinely calls for it and not merely to demonstrate cleverness.
questions: What is a design pattern, and why is it described as a solution rather than finished code?
Into which three categories are the Gang of Four patterns divided, and what does each address?
How does the Strategy pattern remove large conditional statements and support the Open/Closed Principle?
Explain the Observer pattern and give an example of where it is used.
What is the danger of applying patterns unnecessarily, and how should a developer decide to use one?

title: Using frameworks and libraries according to requirements
outline: The difference between a library and a framework.
Reasons to reuse code and the principle of inversion of control.
Selecting a framework or library against requirements.
Dependency management, versioning and package managers.
Risks of dependencies: security, licensing and maintenance.
body: A library is a collection of pre-written, reusable code — functions, classes and modules — that a program calls to perform common tasks such as parsing dates, sending HTTP requests or drawing charts. Using a library saves enormous effort, because the developer does not reinvent well-understood functionality but builds on code that has already been written, tested and refined by others. Almost every non-trivial program depends on many libraries.

A framework is a larger, more opinionated structure that provides a skeleton for an entire class of applications and dictates the overall shape of the program. The crucial distinction between the two is the direction of control. When you use a library, your code is in charge and calls the library when it needs it. When you use a framework, the framework is in charge and calls your code at the points it defines. This reversal is known as inversion of control, sometimes summarized as the 'Hollywood principle': don't call us, we'll call you.

The main reason to use frameworks and libraries is productivity and reliability. Building on mature, widely used components means faster development, fewer bugs in the reused parts, and access to features that would take months to write from scratch, such as security hardening or cross-platform support. It also lets the team concentrate on the unique business logic of their product rather than on solved infrastructural problems.

There are, however, trade-offs. A framework imposes its conventions and structure, so the team must learn and follow its 'way of doing things', which reduces flexibility. Depending on external code also introduces the risk that the dependency has bugs, security holes, or is abandoned by its maintainers. Choosing dependencies is therefore a serious engineering decision, not a casual one, and must be driven by the project's requirements.

Selecting a framework or library begins with the requirements. The developer asks whether the candidate actually solves the required problem, whether it fits the chosen language and platform, and whether it meets non-functional needs such as performance and security. It is short-sighted to pick a tool merely because it is fashionable; the right criterion is fitness for the specified purpose, informed by the constraints of the project and the team's existing skills.

Several practical factors weigh in the choice. The maturity and stability of the project matter, as does the size and activity of its community, because a large community means better documentation, more answered questions and faster bug fixes. The frequency of releases and the responsiveness of maintainers indicate whether the project is alive. Compatibility with the existing technology stack and the learning curve for the team also influence the decision.

The license of a dependency is a decisive and often overlooked factor. Permissive licenses such as MIT and Apache allow almost unrestricted use, whereas copyleft licenses such as the GPL may require that derived work also be released under the same license, which can be unacceptable for proprietary products. A responsible developer checks the license of every dependency to ensure it is compatible with how the software will be distributed, because a licensing violation can have legal consequences.

Modern projects manage their dependencies with a package manager and a manifest file that lists exactly which external packages and versions the project requires. Examples are Maven and Gradle for Java, npm for JavaScript, pip with requirements files or Poetry for Python, and NuGet for .NET. The package manager downloads the declared dependencies, and their transitive dependencies, from a central repository, so the whole team and the build server obtain identical components.

Versions are conventionally expressed with semantic versioning, written as MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. An increase in the patch number signals backward-compatible bug fixes, an increase in the minor number signals backward-compatible new features, and an increase in the major number signals breaking changes that may require the calling code to be modified. Understanding this scheme lets a developer judge how risky it is to upgrade a dependency.

A recurring hazard is transitive dependency conflict, sometimes called 'dependency hell', where two libraries the project needs each require an incompatible version of a third library. Package managers use resolution strategies and lock files to pin exact versions and make builds reproducible, so that the same code always pulls the same dependency tree. Keeping the dependency graph as small and shallow as reasonable reduces the chance of such conflicts.

Dependencies are a major and growing source of security vulnerabilities, because a flaw in a widely used library exposes every application that includes it. Teams must monitor advisories, use automated scanning tools that check the dependency list against databases of known vulnerabilities, and update promptly when a security patch is released. Software Composition Analysis and a Software Bill of Materials — a formal inventory of all included components — are increasingly required in professional practice.

To use a framework well, the developer must invest in learning its conventions, its life cycle and its extension points, usually through the official documentation and tutorials. Fighting a framework by working against its intended patterns leads to fragile, hard-to-maintain code, whereas embracing its conventions yields concise, idiomatic solutions. At the same time, wise design keeps the framework at the edges of the system, so the core business logic does not become hopelessly entangled with a tool that may one day be replaced.

Finally, the decision to add a dependency should be weighed against writing a small amount of code oneself. Pulling in a large library to use one trivial function adds weight, attack surface and maintenance burden out of proportion to the benefit. The balanced professional reuses substantial, well-maintained components for substantial problems, but does not reflexively add a dependency for something a few lines of clear code would solve.

Thus, using frameworks and libraries according to requirements means understanding the difference in control between them, choosing components on the basis of fitness for purpose, community, license and security rather than fashion, managing versions carefully with package managers, and continuously monitoring dependencies for vulnerabilities and maintenance health.
questions: What is the essential difference between a library and a framework, and what does inversion of control mean?
Which criteria should guide the selection of a framework or library for a project?
What does semantic versioning communicate through its MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH numbers?
Why is the license of a dependency an important factor, and how do permissive and copyleft licenses differ?
What security risks arise from external dependencies, and how are they mitigated?

title: Developing mobile applications for the Android and iOS platforms
outline: Characteristics of mobile platforms and their constraints.
Native development for Android and for iOS.
Cross-platform approaches and their trade-offs.
The application life cycle, UI and permissions.
Distribution, testing and mobile-specific concerns.
body: Mobile application development is the creation of software that runs on smartphones and tablets, dominated by two platforms: Android, developed by Google and running on devices from many manufacturers, and iOS, developed by Apple and running only on its own devices. Mobile development differs from desktop and web development because of the special constraints of these devices: limited battery, variable network connectivity, small touch-based screens of many sizes, and a strong emphasis on security and privacy enforced by the platform.

Native Android applications have traditionally been written in Java and now increasingly in Kotlin, which Google endorses as the preferred language. They are built with the Android SDK and the Android Studio IDE, and are compiled and packaged into an APK or the newer AAB bundle. Android's open ecosystem supports a vast range of hardware, which brings the challenge of fragmentation: the developer must ensure the application works across many device sizes, manufacturers and versions of the operating system.

Native iOS applications are written in Swift, Apple's modern language, or in the older Objective-C, using the Xcode IDE and Apple's frameworks. Because Apple controls both the hardware and the software, the range of devices is smaller and more uniform, which simplifies testing, but development requires a Mac computer and enrolment in the Apple Developer Program. Each platform provides its own user-interface toolkit — Jetpack Compose or XML layouts on Android, SwiftUI or UIKit on iOS — reflecting distinct design conventions.

Writing and maintaining two separate native codebases is expensive, so cross-platform frameworks aim to share one codebase across both platforms. React Native, backed by Meta, lets developers build mobile apps with JavaScript and React. Flutter, from Google, uses the Dart language and renders its own widgets for a consistent look on both platforms. Kotlin Multiplatform shares business logic while allowing native user interfaces. These tools trade some access to the very latest native features and peak performance for a large gain in development speed and code reuse.

The choice between native and cross-platform development is a requirements-driven decision. Native development gives the best performance, immediate access to new platform features and the most polished platform-specific feel, and is preferred for graphically intensive apps such as games or apps that use specialized hardware. Cross-platform development is chosen when time-to-market, budget and a single team maintaining both platforms outweigh the need for maximum performance or the newest native capabilities.

A defining feature of mobile programming is the activity or view-controller life cycle. Unlike a desktop program that runs until the user closes it, a mobile app is constantly created, paused, resumed and destroyed by the operating system as the user switches apps, receives a call or rotates the screen. Android expresses this through life-cycle callbacks such as onCreate, onPause and onDestroy, and iOS through analogous methods. The developer must save and restore state at the right moments, or the app will lose data or crash when interrupted.

Because the operating system can terminate a backgrounded app at any time to reclaim resources, mobile developers must be disciplined about state and memory. Long-running work must be delegated to background services or scheduled jobs that respect battery and data-saving policies, and heavy operations must never block the main UI thread, or the system will show an 'application not responding' warning. Efficient use of battery, memory and network is a first-class requirement, not an afterthought.

User interfaces on mobile are touch-first and must adapt to a wide range of screen sizes and orientations, so layouts are built to be responsive rather than fixed. Both platforms provide layout systems and design guidelines — Material Design for Android and the Human Interface Guidelines for iOS — that codify the expected look and interaction so that apps feel native to their platform. Respecting these conventions is important both for usability and for approval on the app stores.

Mobile apps access sensitive capabilities — the camera, location, contacts, microphone and storage — only with the user's permission. Modern platforms require the app to request these permissions at run time and to explain why they are needed, and the user can deny or revoke them at any time. The developer must handle the case where a permission is refused gracefully, and must follow privacy rules, collecting only the data the app genuinely needs, because both stores reject apps that mishandle personal data.

Distribution is tightly controlled compared with the web. Android apps are published mainly through the Google Play Store, and iOS apps exclusively through Apple's App Store, each of which imposes a review process, content policies and technical requirements before an app is accepted. Apps must be cryptographically signed, and Apple in particular enforces strict guidelines. Understanding these publication requirements is part of developing an app 'per requirements', because a technically working app that violates store policy cannot be released.

Testing mobile applications adds the dimension of device diversity. Beyond ordinary unit and integration tests, developers use UI-testing frameworks such as Espresso on Android and XCTest on iOS, and run the app on emulators and simulators as well as real devices. Cloud-based device farms allow testing across dozens of real device models, and staged rollouts and beta channels such as TestFlight let a limited group of users try a release before it reaches everyone.

Most mobile apps are only the front end of a larger system, communicating with a back-end server through APIs, so much of what was studied about integration, security and offline behaviour applies directly. A well-designed app caches data locally so it remains usable without connectivity, synchronizes changes when the network returns, and secures data both in transit, using encrypted connections, and at rest on the device, because a phone is easily lost or stolen.

Thus, developing mobile applications for Android and iOS requires understanding each platform's languages, tools and design conventions, choosing between native and cross-platform approaches according to the requirements, respecting the app life cycle and the tight limits on battery, memory and permissions, and meeting the strict testing, security and store-publication rules that distinguish mobile development from other kinds of programming.
questions: Which languages and tools are used for native Android and native iOS development?
What are the trade-offs between native and cross-platform development, and when is each preferred?
Why is the activity or view-controller life cycle so important in mobile programming?
How do modern platforms handle access to sensitive capabilities such as the camera or location?
What special requirements must be met to distribute an app through the Google Play Store and the Apple App Store?

title: Creating web applications and developing the server side
outline: Architecture of a web application: client, server and database.
The request-response cycle and the HTTP protocol.
Server-side responsibilities and technologies.
Data persistence, sessions and authentication.
Scalability, security and deployment of web applications.
body: A web application is a program that runs across a network and is used through a web browser, splitting its work between a client running in the browser and a server running on a remote machine. The client, or front end, is responsible for what the user sees and interacts with; the server, or back end, is responsible for the business logic, data storage and security. This separation lets many users share one central system while keeping the sensitive logic and data safely on the server.

The whole of the web is built on the request-response cycle over the HTTP protocol. The browser sends an HTTP request identifying a resource by its URL and a method such as GET or POST, and the server processes it and returns an HTTP response containing a status code and a body. HTTP is stateless, meaning each request is independent and the server does not automatically remember previous requests; maintaining the illusion of a continuous session is one of the server's key jobs, addressed later in this lecture.

The front end of a web application is built from three core technologies: HTML defines the structure and content of a page, CSS defines its appearance and layout, and JavaScript adds interactivity in the browser. Modern front ends often use frameworks such as React, Angular or Vue to build rich single-page applications, in which JavaScript updates the page dynamically and communicates with the server through API calls rather than reloading whole pages. Regardless of sophistication, the front end must never be trusted to enforce security, because the user controls the browser.

The server side is where the core of a web application lives, and it has several responsibilities: receiving and validating requests, executing business logic, reading and writing the database, enforcing security and authentication, and producing the response. Because the server is under the developer's control and the client is not, all critical rules — such as checking that a user is allowed to perform an action or that an input is valid — must be enforced on the server, even if the client also checks them for convenience.

Many languages and frameworks are used to build server-side applications, each with a rich ecosystem. Common choices include Node.js with Express in JavaScript, Django and Flask in Python, Spring Boot in Java, ASP.NET Core in C#, Ruby on Rails, and Laravel in PHP. These frameworks provide routing that maps URLs to handler functions, tools for reading requests and building responses, and integrations for databases, security and templating, so the developer can focus on the application's specific logic.

Almost every web application must store data permanently, and the server is responsible for this persistence. Relational databases such as PostgreSQL and MySQL store structured data in tables and are queried with SQL, offering strong consistency and transactions; NoSQL databases such as MongoDB store flexible documents and scale horizontally for large or loosely structured data. The server communicates with the database, often through an object-relational mapper that translates between program objects and database rows, and must protect against injection attacks by using parameterized queries.

Because HTTP is stateless, the server needs a mechanism to recognize a returning user and remember that they have logged in. Traditionally this is done with sessions: on login the server creates a session record and sends the browser a cookie holding a session identifier, which the browser returns on every subsequent request. An increasingly common alternative, especially for APIs, is token-based authentication such as JSON Web Tokens, where the client presents a signed token that the server can verify without storing session state, which suits distributed and mobile clients.

Authentication and authorization are distinct and both essential. Authentication verifies who the user is, typically by checking a password, which the server must store only as a salted cryptographic hash, never in plain text. Authorization then decides what that authenticated user is allowed to do, often through roles and permissions. Getting these wrong is among the most damaging security failures, so frameworks provide well-tested modules that developers should use rather than inventing their own.

Web applications are exposed to the whole internet, so security is a constant concern. The OWASP Top Ten catalogues the most common risks, including injection attacks, broken authentication, cross-site scripting in which malicious script is injected into pages, and cross-site request forgery. Defences include validating and sanitizing all input, encoding output, using parameterized database queries, always serving traffic over encrypted HTTPS, and applying the principle of least privilege so each component has only the access it needs.

As a web application grows popular it must scale to serve more users. Vertical scaling means giving one server more power, but it has limits; horizontal scaling means adding more server instances behind a load balancer that distributes requests among them. Horizontal scaling works best when the server is stateless, so any instance can handle any request, which is one reason token-based authentication and external session or cache stores such as Redis are attractive. Caching frequently requested data further reduces load and improves response time.

The application programming interface is the contract between front end and back end and between separate services. Well-designed web back ends expose RESTful or GraphQL APIs that return data, usually as JSON, which the front end and other clients consume. This clean separation means the same server can serve a web front end, a mobile app and third-party integrations at once, and it connects directly to the earlier lecture on integration with external systems and APIs.

Finally, a web application must be deployed and operated reliably. Modern practice packages the application into containers with Docker so it runs identically everywhere, orchestrates many containers with tools such as Kubernetes, and automates building, testing and releasing through continuous-integration and continuous-deployment pipelines. Cloud platforms provide the underlying servers, databases and networking on demand, and monitoring and logging let the team observe the running system and respond to problems quickly.

Thus, creating a web application means dividing responsibility between a browser-based client and a server that owns the business logic, data and security, mastering the stateless request-response cycle over HTTP, choosing appropriate server-side and database technologies, enforcing authentication and defending against web-specific attacks, and deploying the result in a way that can scale and be operated reliably.
questions: How are responsibilities divided between the client and the server in a web application?
Why is HTTP described as stateless, and how do sessions or tokens maintain a logged-in user?
Why must all critical validation and security checks be performed on the server rather than the client?
What is the difference between authentication and authorization, and how should passwords be stored?
What does horizontal scaling require of the server, and how does a load balancer support it?

title: Integrating and using artificial intelligence in code
outline: What AI integration means and its main forms.
Using pre-trained models and cloud AI services.
Large language models and calling them through APIs.
Prompt design, context and retrieval-augmented generation.
Practical, ethical and reliability considerations.
body: Integrating artificial intelligence into code means adding capabilities to an application that would traditionally require human judgement — recognizing images, understanding and generating natural language, making recommendations, or predicting outcomes — by using machine-learning models. For the great majority of developers, this does not mean building and training models from scratch, which requires large datasets and specialized expertise, but rather consuming existing models through libraries and services and weaving their results into the application's logic.

There are several common forms of AI integration. A developer may call a cloud AI service over an API, such as a vision service that labels the contents of a photograph or a speech service that transcribes audio. They may embed a pre-trained model directly in the application and run inference locally. Or they may train or fine-tune a model on their own data using a machine-learning library. The right form depends on the requirements: data sensitivity, latency, cost and how specialized the task is.

Cloud AI services are the fastest path to intelligent features. Providers expose ready-made models for vision, speech, translation and text analysis behind ordinary web APIs, so the developer sends data and receives a structured result, paying per use. This requires no machine-learning expertise and connects directly to the earlier lecture on API integration, since the same skills of authentication, error handling and rate-limiting apply. The trade-off is dependence on the provider, ongoing cost, and sending data outside the organization.

When a team does run its own models, popular frameworks include TensorFlow and PyTorch for building and training neural networks, and scikit-learn for classical machine learning such as regression and decision trees. A trained model can be exported in an interchange format such as ONNX and loaded into a program for inference. The essential distinction is between training, the expensive one-time process of learning from data, and inference, the cheap repeated process of applying the trained model to new inputs, which is what most applications actually do at run time.

The most transformative recent development is the large language model, a model trained on vast text corpora that can understand and generate human-like language. Developers integrate such models to build features like chat assistants, text summarization, classification, code generation and data extraction. In practice this is done by calling the model provider's API: the program sends a textual prompt and receives generated text, often streamed token by token so the user sees the answer appear progressively.

The quality of what a language model produces depends heavily on the prompt, so prompt design has become a practical engineering skill. A good prompt states the task clearly, provides relevant context and any required format for the answer, and may include a few examples of the desired input-output pairs, a technique called few-shot prompting. Because the model has a limited context window — a maximum amount of text it can consider at once — the developer must supply the most relevant information concisely rather than dumping everything.

A powerful pattern for grounding a language model in an organization's own knowledge is retrieval-augmented generation, or RAG. Instead of relying only on what the model learned during training, the application first searches a knowledge base for passages relevant to the user's question, then inserts those passages into the prompt as context, and finally asks the model to answer using them. This lets the model answer questions about private, up-to-date documents and greatly reduces invented answers, because the response is anchored to retrieved facts.

Beyond single calls, developers build agents and tool use, in which the language model is allowed to call functions the developer defines — such as querying a database, performing a calculation or fetching a web page — and then incorporate the results into its reasoning. This turns the model from a text generator into a component that can take actions within a controlled boundary. The developer must define the available tools carefully and validate everything the model asks to do, because the model's requests are suggestions, not commands to be trusted blindly.

AI components differ fundamentally from ordinary code in that they are probabilistic rather than deterministic: the same input may produce different outputs, and the output may be confidently wrong. Language models in particular can 'hallucinate', producing plausible but false statements. Therefore an application must never treat model output as automatically correct; it should validate results, constrain them to expected formats, keep a human in the loop for consequential decisions, and design the user experience to make the AI's uncertainty visible.

Practical engineering concerns surround every AI integration. API calls to large models have real monetary cost and noticeable latency, so developers cache responses where possible, choose a model whose capability matches the task rather than always using the largest, and stream long responses for a better user experience. Robust error handling and timeouts matter as much here as in any integration, and usage should be monitored to control cost and detect abuse.

Ethical and legal responsibilities are central to AI in code, not optional. Models can reflect and amplify biases present in their training data, producing unfair outcomes, so developers must test for bias in high-stakes applications. Privacy must be protected: sensitive user data sent to an external model may be stored or used, so the team must understand the provider's data policy and avoid sending personal or confidential information without safeguards. Transparency with users about when they are interacting with an AI is increasingly a legal as well as an ethical requirement.

Security introduces new risks specific to AI. Prompt injection is an attack in which malicious text hidden in user input or in retrieved documents tries to override the developer's instructions and make the model misbehave. Because the boundary between instructions and data is blurred in language models, developers must treat all external text as untrusted, restrict the model's tools and permissions, and never let model output directly trigger dangerous actions without validation. Defending AI features requires the same suspicion of input as any other web-facing code.

Thus, integrating artificial intelligence into code most often means consuming pre-trained models and services through APIs, shaping their behaviour with well-designed prompts and retrieval of relevant context, and doing so with full awareness that AI output is probabilistic and fallible — which demands validation, cost and latency management, and careful attention to bias, privacy, transparency and new security risks such as prompt injection.
questions: What are the main forms of AI integration, and why do most developers use pre-trained models rather than train their own?
What is the difference between training and inference, and which do most applications perform at run time?
What is retrieval-augmented generation, and what problem does it solve?
Why must the output of a language model never be treated as automatically correct?
What is prompt injection, and how should developers defend against it?

title: Developing code for big-data processing and data analysis
outline: What 'big data' means and the challenges it poses.
The data-processing pipeline: ingest, store, process, analyse.
Batch versus stream processing and distributed frameworks.
Programming models: MapReduce, DataFrames and SQL on big data.
Data quality, analysis and responsible use.
body: Big data refers to datasets so large, fast-arriving or varied that traditional single-machine tools and databases cannot process them efficiently. The concept is often summarized by three Vs: volume, the sheer size of the data, often measured in terabytes or petabytes; velocity, the speed at which new data arrives, sometimes as a continuous stream; and variety, the mixture of structured, semi-structured and unstructured forms such as tables, logs, text, images and sensor readings. Some add veracity, the uncertainty and noise in the data, and value, the useful insight to be extracted.

The central difficulty is that the data no longer fits or processes on one machine, which forces a shift from single-machine programming to distributed computing. The core idea is to split the data across many machines in a cluster and process the pieces in parallel, then combine the results. This lets computation scale horizontally by adding more machines, but it introduces new problems: coordinating the machines, tolerating the failure of individual nodes, and moving data efficiently across the network.

Data-processing work is usually organized as a pipeline with distinct stages. First, ingestion collects data from sources such as databases, logs, sensors or external APIs. Second, storage places the raw data in a scalable store. Third, processing cleans, transforms and aggregates the data. Fourth, analysis and visualization extract insight and present it. Designing code for big data means thinking in terms of these stages and the flow of data between them, rather than a single monolithic program.

Where big data is stored differs from ordinary applications. A distributed file system such as the Hadoop Distributed File System spreads large files across many machines with replication for fault tolerance. A data lake stores vast amounts of raw data in its native form for later processing, while a data warehouse such as those built on columnar databases stores cleaned, structured data optimized for analytical queries. Cloud object stores now commonly serve as the underlying storage layer for all of these.

A fundamental design choice is between batch and stream processing. Batch processing collects data over a period and processes it in large chunks — for example, computing yesterday's sales every night — which is efficient and simple but introduces delay. Stream processing handles each event as it arrives, giving near real-time results needed for fraud detection or live dashboards, at the cost of greater complexity. Many modern systems combine both in a single architecture, and frameworks increasingly unify the two programming models.
